value: Headline: The latest EIA report delivered a blockbuster 8.3 mb crude draw for the week ending June 12, 2026, but yesterday's tape faded the bullish signal — WTI settled lower at $73.00 as Hormuz risk premium leaked out of the front. Cushing's collapse to 20.0 mb — already below the ~25 mb operational floor — remains the structural story underpinning the whole curve, even as flat price softens. The market is wrestling with a genuinely tight physical setup against macro and geopolitical crosswinds. Crude & products: Per last week's WPSR (week ending June 12, 2026), commercial crude drew 8.3 mb to 418.2 mb as refiners ran flat-out — utilization climbed 1.4 points to 96.7% and crude inputs hit 17.19 mb/d. The draw was front-loaded in PADD II (-4.5 mb) and PADD III (-4.8 mb) as Gulf Coast and Midwest plants pushed near peak, with the 4-week export pace easing to 4.87 mb/d from 5.57 mb/d. Cushing fell another 1.6 mb to 20.0 mb — operating below tank-bottom minimums and down 5.8 mb over five weeks from 25.82 mb on May 15. On the product side, gasoline drew 0.9 mb to 214.2 mb and jet fell 1.1 mb to 45.0 mb, while distillates built a modest 1.0 mb to 103.1 mb. Refining margins: Margins remain robust, consistent with the 96.7% run rate. The 3:2:1 crack sits at $50.27, with the distillate crack the standout at $57.89 and gasoline at $46.45. Yesterday's price action favored the diesel barrel — ULSD settled up 6 c/gal to $3.1165 while RBOB slipped 4 c/gal to $2.8441 — a constructive regrade signal that gains weight against Novak's diesel-export-ban headline (). With jet stocks drawing and the jet crack at $42.32, the distillate complex is doing the heavy lifting for refiner economics. Curve structure: WTI holds firm backwardation — the M1-M2 spread is $0.64 and M1-M12 is $4.63, signaling the front-end tightness Cushing's sub-floor inventory implies. Brent backwardation is shallower at $0.28 M1-M2 and $3.78 M1-M12. The Brent-WTI spread sits narrow at $3.87, a weak export arb that keeps barrels home and pressures domestic crude — yet the Cushing drawdown is overriding that signal, so the front WTI structure stays tight despite softening exports. Watch for the arb to need a wider spread (WTI lower or Brent higher) before US export volumes recover. News & geopolitics: The dominant macro thread remains the Strait of Hormuz: stalled US-Iran peace talks and escalating naval-blockade fears across Saudi Arabia, Kuwait, UAE and Iraq are keeping a war premium in the market, while tanker owners are reportedly logging their best week of the crisis as freight rates spike. Russia's Novak floated a complete ban on diesel exports — directly bullish for global distillate and supportive of that $57.89 ULSD crack. Meanwhile, traders are openly questioning how much sanctioned Iranian crude could realistically return to market, tempering bearish supply narratives, even as China lines up a second LNG terminal for sanctioned Russian cargoes. Domestically, the EIA flagged that US crude inventories continue to falter with the SPR struggling to refill, while the Trump administration moves to cut drilling costs — a longer-horizon supply tailwind that does nothing for today's sub-floor Cushing reality.
